summary: Evans challenges conference organizers who claim they have no control over the diversity of their talk submissions. She argues that active outreach to underrepresented groups, combined with helping people improve their proposals, directly increases both the quantity and quality of diverse submissions. She cites PyCon's dramatic increase in women speakers (from 1% to 33% over four years) as proof that deliberate outreach works. The post frames diversity in conference lineups as a choice organizers actively make or fail to make.
key_points: Conference organizers choose who submits proposals through active outreach and encouragement
Helping speakers refine their proposals dramatically improves submission quality, not just quantity
PyCon's women speaker ratio went from 1% in 2011 to 33% in 2014 through deliberate diversity outreach
Personal invitations to speak are far more effective than passive open calls for proposals
money_quotes: We have a choice about who submits talk proposals, and also about who submits the *best* talk proposals.
If you ask someone specifically to consider speaking at your conference, they're WAY more likely to consider submitting a talk than if you don't.
If you then actively work with some talk submitters to help them focus and improve the talk they submit, their proposals will get better!
% PyCon talks by women: (2011: 1%), (2012: 7%), (2013: 15%), (2014: 33%). Outreach works.
Telling people that I like their work and that I'd like for them to talk about it is super fun! (and true!)
key_insight: Conference diversity isn't a matter of luck or pipeline — it's the direct result of organizers choosing to do personal outreach and help underrepresented speakers craft strong proposals.
